planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; bear huckleberry grows naturally in acidic woodland understories and high-elevation forests of the southern Appalachians.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5-5.5), well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial shade; bear huckleberry is a deciduous native shrub of the high-elevation Appalachian forests from North Carolina to Tennessee, naturally growing in the understory beneath oaks, hemlocks, and rhododendrons. The shiny black berries ripen in late summer and are eaten by bears (which explains the common name), black bears being primary dispersers in its mountain habitat. The berries are edible by humans as well — sweet and flavorful, similar to blueberries but with a distinctive tang. Like all huckleberries, the plant is challenging to cultivate outside its specific acidic, woodland conditions; it does not tolerate alkaline soil, dry soil, or full sun. Excellent for native woodland gardens in the southern mountains.

planting.note: Plant container stock in fall in well-drained, sandy, strongly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; small black blueberry is native to the sandy pine flatwoods and longleaf pine savannas of the Gulf Coastal Plain and requires the infertile, acidic, well-drained conditions of that habitat.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, strongly acidic (pH 4.5-5.5), lean soil in partial shade to full sun; small black blueberry is a dwarf, spreading native vaccinium of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain pine flatwoods, closely associated with longleaf pine habitat. The small, very dark blue-black berries ripen in early summer and, while tiny, are intensely flavored and sweet — eaten fresh or used in preserves and baked goods in the traditional Southern way. The plant spreads slowly by rhizomes to form low colonies and provides important food for dozens of bird species. In the native range, it grows in the precise habitat type (sandy, dry, very acidic) that is among the most threatened ecosystems in North America. An excellent species for naturalized longleaf pine ecosystem restoration in the Gulf Coastal Plain.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Japanese ligularia wilts dramatically in heat and afternoon sun — situate where it receives morning sun and afternoon shade, and ensure consistent moisture.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade with protection from afternoon sun and heat; Japanese ligularia produces large, deeply cut, ornate leaves with incised lobes that give the plant a lacy, highly ornamental texture quite distinct from the rounder-leaved L. dentata. Tall spires of bright orange-yellow daisy flowers rise in summer. Like all ligularias, it wilts dramatically in midday sun and heat, looking devastated on hot afternoons, then recovering fully by morning — this is normal drought-stress behavior, not a sign of disease, but it can be unnerving. Best sited where it receives morning sun and afternoon shade with consistently moist, cool soil. Mulch heavily to keep roots cool. Excellent for Asian-style woodland gardens alongside hostas and ferns.

planting.note: Use fresh seeds — angelica seeds lose viability quickly. Cold-stratify for 2-4 weeks before sowing indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant to deep, humus-rich, consistently moist soil in partial shade. Roots are harvested in fall of the 3rd year.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; dong quai is one of the most important herbs in Traditional Chinese Medicine and ranks among the top-selling botanical supplements globally. The fleshy taproot, harvested in the fall of the third year of growth, has been used for over 2000 years in Chinese medicine primarily for women's reproductive health — regulating menstruation, easing menopausal symptoms, and treating anemia — earning the common name 'female ginseng.' Modern research confirms phytoestrogenic and blood-moving compounds in the root. Use only under guidance of a healthcare professional as it interacts with blood-thinning medications. The plant is a biennial to short-lived perennial that dies after flowering; remove flower stalks as they appear to extend the life of the root. Partial shade and consistent moisture are essential.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial shade; Japanese clethra is more tree-like than the native summersweet (C. alnifolia) and suits larger garden spaces. Propagate from softwood cuttings in summer.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese clethra is a multi-stemmed large shrub or small tree from Japan that surpasses the popular native summersweet (C. alnifolia) in ornamental impact at a larger scale. The most distinctive feature is the exfoliating, cinnamon-colored bark that peels in attractive layers on mature stems — providing striking winter interest. The long, arching racemes of fragrant white flowers in midsummer attract countless butterflies and bees. Excellent fall color in shades of yellow and red. More tolerant of dry soil than C. alnifolia once established. An underused, three-season ornamental shrub for shaded woodland gardens, native plant borders, and naturalized settings.
